name: Paulo Freire
bio: Paulo Freire was a Brazilian educator and philosopher, widely regarded as one of the most influential educational thinkers of the 20th century. Born in Recife in 1921, he experienced poverty and hunger during the Great Depression, which deeply shaped his later work. Freire developed a critical approach to education that challenged the traditional 'banking model' of teaching, where students are treated as empty vessels to be filled with knowledge. Instead, he advocated for a dialogical, problem-posing pedagogy that empowers learners to critically engage with their social reality and become agents of transformation. His seminal work, 'Pedagogy of the Oppressed' (1968), became a foundational text for critical pedagogy, liberation theology, and social movements worldwide. Freire’s method emphasizes literacy as a tool for conscientization—the process of developing critical consciousness about social, political, and economic contradictions. He worked extensively in adult literacy campaigns in Brazil and later in Chile, Guinea-Bissau, and other countries. Exiled after the 1964 Brazilian coup, he continued his work internationally, serving as a consultant for UNESCO and teaching at Harvard University. Freire’s ideas have profoundly influenced education, sociology, postcolonial studies, and activism, advocating for education as a practice of freedom rather than domination. He passed away in 1997, leaving a legacy that continues to inspire educators and movements for social justice around the globe.
